levels.label: Unmapped
levels.copy: The Responsible AI & EU/Swiss Readiness pattern is still mostly informal or ad hoc. Pick one stabilising action, document it, and avoid adding a long process before the basics are real.

levels.label: Aware
levels.copy: Some Responsible AI & EU/Swiss Readiness practices exist, but they still depend on individual memory and effort. The next step is two repeatable routines with clear owners.

levels.label: Controlled
levels.copy: Core Responsible AI & EU/Swiss Readiness practices are repeatable, but evidence quality and ownership are uneven. Work on the weakest dimension before optimising the strongest one.

levels.label: Audit-Ready
levels.copy: Responsible AI & EU/Swiss Readiness practices are measured and connected to decisions. The main work is now removing the constraint that prevents the system from scaling cleanly.

levels.label: Trustworthy-by-Design
levels.copy: Responsible AI & EU/Swiss Readiness is operating as a learning system. Keep calibrating against evidence, stress-test resilience, and avoid treating a strong score as a permanent guarantee.

recommendations.classification-and-scope: Build the AI inventory and document role, jurisdiction, risk tier, owner, and change triggers.
recommendations.data-and-privacy: Map data provenance, purpose, access, retention, transfers, and rights for the highest-risk system.
recommendations.risk-and-human-oversight: Define a risk-based evaluation suite and test whether human oversight can actually prevent or correct harm.
recommendations.documentation-and-monitoring: Create an evidence pack with system card, instructions, logs, evaluations, changes, incidents, and monitoring thresholds.

defaultSecondAction: Retake the diagnostic after a focused 30-day improvement cycle and compare only your own progress unless a team comparison is explicitly agreed.
reflectionQuestion: Which assumption behind your weakest dimension deserves the fastest evidence check?
methodologyNote: The current version uses four dimensions with three questions each. Dimension scores are normalised to 0-100. The balanced index weights 70% average dimension performance and 30% the weakest dimension. No percentile or benchmark is shown until a compatible sample exists.
shareNote: Share cards reveal only the level and strongest area. They do not publish individual answers, sensitive weaknesses, legal or financial details, security gaps, or family-specific information.
